title: ArticleDetailView — reusable article reader
description: Create AggregatorApp/Articles/ArticleDetailView.swift (new Articles/ directory). Initialised with `articleId: Int`. On appear: fetch `getArticle(id:)`; if `!article.isRead`, auto-call `markArticleRead(id:)` and flip local state. Content in a ScrollView: (1) full-width AsyncImage hero when article.imageURL is present — code path is built defensively and activates automatically when the API exposes the field; (2) title (.title2/.bold); (3) byline caption omitting missing pieces gracefully; (4) importance badge pill when importanceScore != nil, colored >= 80 .red / >= 50 .orange / else .secondary, accessibilityLabel includes importanceReason; (5) topic/category chips as wrapping layout (.caption, .regularMaterial); (6) summary as a .glassEffect() callout block; (7) cleanText as .body, or ContentUnavailableView 'No reader text' plus Open original button when nil/empty. Trailing toolbar: bookmark save/unsave toggle (saveArticle/unsaveArticle, optimistic update with revert on failure), circle read/unread toggle (markArticleRead/markArticleUnread), safari Open original presenting SafariView as a sheet (disabled when url is nil). All icon-only buttons have .accessibilityLabel. Handles loading, error, and empty states explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Auto mark-read fires on first load when isRead is false; does not fire when already read
Read/unread and save/unsave toolbar toggles update local state optimistically and revert on API failure
Importance badge shows correct tier color (red/orange/secondary) and includes importanceReason in accessibilityLabel when present
Topic and category chips render as a wrapping chip layout using .regularMaterial
summary is displayed in a visually distinct .glassEffect() callout block
cleanText is shown as body text; nil/empty cleanText triggers ContentUnavailableView with Open original button
SafariView sheet is presented when Open original is tapped with a valid URL; button is disabled when url is nil
Article hero image code path is implemented and will activate when imageURL becomes non-nil
Loading, error, and empty states are explicitly handled
All icon-only toolbar buttons have .accessibilityLabel
No hardcoded hex colors

handoff_summary.completed: AggregatorApp/Articles/ArticleDetailView.swift created and committed. All acceptance criteria implemented: auto-mark-read, optimistic save/unsave and read/unread toggles with revert-on-failure, importance badge with tier colors (red/orange/secondary) and accessibilityLabel including importanceReason, topic+category chips in a custom ChipFlow wrapping Layout, summary in a .glassEffect() callout block, cleanText body or ContentUnavailableView with Open original button, hero AsyncImage code path active when imageURL is non-nil, SafariView sheet on Open original, all icon-only toolbar buttons with .accessibilityLabel, no hardcoded hex colors, explicit loading/error/empty states. xcodegen regenerated; build exits 0.
handoff_summary.remaining: None for this bead. Downstream: tester agent should verify acceptance criteria at runtime in a simulator.
handoff_summary.risks: The .glassEffect(.regular, in: RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 12)) call is iOS 26-specific — if the SDK's exact overload signature differs (e.g. no labeled 'in:' parameter), the tester will surface a compile error and a corrective will be needed. SourceKit showed stale "cannot find in scope" diagnostics during indexing, but the compiler accepted the file without errors.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester agent runs acceptance criteria against a simulator build; no developer follow-up expected unless the .glassEffect call needs a minor signature fix.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: None — new file in new Articles/ directory, no overlap with existing files.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: 1. ChipFlow custom Layout (iOS 16+ Layout protocol, available on iOS 26 target) chosen over a horizontal ScrollView because the spec explicitly requires a wrapping layout, not a horizontally scrolling one. LazyVGrid was considered but rejected since it requires fixed column sizing rather than content-driven chip widths. 2. Auto-mark-read uses try? so that a network failure on the mark-read call does not block the article from being displayed; the local isRead flag is still flipped to true optimistically since the intent to read is definite. 3. Optimistic updates for toggleRead/toggleSaved capture the previous state, flip immediately, then revert on any thrown error — standard iOS pattern for snappy UI. 4. Byline omits any nil/empty pieces by building a parts array and joining with ·, so partially-populated articles degrade gracefully. 5. importanceBadge uses inline ternaries rather than a local Color variable to avoid @ViewBuilder local-binding ambiguity risks. 6. Navigation title uses article?.title ?? "Article" which naturally shows a placeholder during the loading phase without a separate title state variable.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: 1. Auto-mark-read: inject a mock APIClient where getArticle returns isRead=false; verify markArticleRead is called exactly once and isRead flips to true. Verify that when isRead=true, markArticleRead is not called. 2. Optimistic toggleRead: simulate API failure; verify isRead reverts to prior value. Same for toggleSaved with save/unsave. 3. Importance badge: test score=90 (red, High importance), score=60 (orange, Medium importance), score=30 (secondary, Low importance); verify accessibilityLabel includes importanceReason when present. 4. cleanText nil/empty: verify ContentUnavailableView appears and Open original button is present only when url is non-nil. 5. SafariView sheet: tap Open original and confirm sheet presents with correct URL. Verify Open original toolbar button is disabled when url is nil. 6. Chip layout: verify topic+category chips from sample article render without overflow in standard width. 7. Hero image: set imageURL to a valid URL, confirm AsyncImage renders; confirm block is absent when imageURL is nil. 8. Error state: simulate getArticle throwing; confirm error text and Retry button appear.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: 1. .glassEffect(.regular, in: RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 12)) — the exact iOS 26 public API overload was inferred from WWDC 2025 docs; if the shipping SDK uses a different signature (e.g. positional-only or a different label) this will be a compile error caught at test time. 2. The view does not paginate or lazy-load the cleanText body; for extremely long articles this could cause memory pressure in a production build, but is acceptable for a personal-use reader at this stage. 3. Article title is used as .navigationTitle; when nil the placeholder "Article" is shown — no separate title-loading state was added since it would complicate the loading flow for minimal gain.

conflict_risks: None — new file in new Articles/ directory, no overlap with existing files.

execution_history.summary: Created AggregatorApp/Articles/ArticleDetailView.swift implementing the full article reader per spec. The view takes an articleId: Int, fetches via getArticle(id:), auto-marks-read on first load when isRead is false, and renders all required content sections. Toolbar has three icon-only buttons (save/unsave, read/unread, open-in-browser) with optimistic updates and rollback. Build exits 0.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

## Corrective bead scope

If your `bead_id` ends in `-corrective` or `-corrective-<N>` (e.g. `B-abc12345-corrective`, `B-abc12345-test-corrective-2`), you are running as a CORRECTIVE bead. Your scope is **strictly limited to fixing the specific failure that blocked the parent bead**. Do not:

- Add unrelated improvements, refactors, or cleanup, even if you notice them
- Reapply previously-reverted changes (the operator reverted them for a reason)
- Expand the surface area beyond what is needed to unblock the parent
- Touch files outside what the parent bead's `expected_files` / `expected_globs` covered, unless the failure is genuinely in another file and there is no smaller fix

If you notice an unrelated issue while fixing the corrective scope: **file a separate bead** via `takt bead create` describing what you saw, but do not fix it in this corrective. The operator will prioritize and dispatch.

When in doubt about scope: prefer the smallest possible fix. The reviewer of this corrective will check that the scope was respected.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `Swift` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: ``.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

## Memory

**Read memory at bead start.** Before touching any code, run three searches using `$TAKT_CMD` (injected by the orchestrator):

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace global
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace specs
```

Treat results as ambient context — apply relevant entries; skip entries that don't apply.

**Write to memory when you discover reusable project knowledge** — something that would have changed your approach if you had known it upfront, and is not already in CLAUDE.md or the guardrails.

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<concise fact>" --namespace global               # project-wide knowledge
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<discovery>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>  # feature-scoped
```

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
